summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the overall balanced equation for aerobic cellular respiration?

The balanced equation for aerobic cellular respiration is C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + energy (approximately 36–38 ATP). Glucose is oxidized and oxygen is reduced, releasing chemical energy that is captured in ATP.

What is the approximate total ATP yield from the complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule?

The complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule yields approximately 36–38 ATP: 2 from glycolysis, 2 from the Krebs cycle, and about 32–34 from the electron transport chain / oxidative phosphorylation. The majority of ATP comes from the ETC.

What is the final electron acceptor in the electron transport chain?

Oxygen (O₂) is the final (terminal) electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. After electrons pass through the chain, they combine with O₂ and H⁺ ions to form water (H₂O). This is why cellular respiration requires oxygen and is called 'aerobic.'

Cyanide is a lethal poison that binds irreversibly to Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) of the electron transport chain. If a patient is exposed to cyanide, what is the immediate biochemical consequence?

Cyanide blocks Complex IV, preventing electron transfer to O₂. Since no electrons can exit the chain, all upstream carriers (NADH, FADH₂, ubiquinone, cytochromes) remain reduced, the proton gradient dissipates, and ATP synthase stops. The Krebs cycle also stalls because NAD⁺ and FAD cannot be regenerated. Cells are forced to rely on glycolysis alone (2 ATP), which is insufficient for most tissues, especially the brain.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: What are the three main stages of aerobic cellular respiration, in order?

Aerobic cellular respiration proceeds through three main stages: (1) glycolysis in the cytoplasm, (2) the Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle) in the mitochondrial matrix, and (3) the electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation on the inner mitochondrial membrane.
